guide_description: Five days covering Rome's essentials plus the bohemian west bank and the green northeast. Each day is geographically self-contained.

days.cluster_tagline: Baroque squares and ancient temples in a walkable historic core
days.neighborhood_description: The dense historic quarter between the Tiber bend and Via del Corso. Roman temples, baroque piazzas, and market squares stacked on top of two millennia of street grid.

morning.notes: Arrive at opening (9am) to see the oculus beam land cleanly on the interior before the floor fills with tour groups. A small entry fee now applies on weekdays; Sunday mass is free but limits movement inside.

afternoon.activity_title: Piazza Navona and Sant'Agnese in Agone
afternoon.notes: Built on the footprint of the Stadium of Domitian, which is why the shape is elongated. The Fountain of the Four Rivers is Bernini; the church facade opposite is by his rival Borromini - the two have been arguing across the piazza since the 1650s.

evening.notes: Lighting comes on around sunset and the fountain is visibly cleaner than the daytime selfie scrum suggests. Approach from Via della Stamperia rather than the main funnel street for the reveal Fellini used in La Dolce Vita.

if_time_permits.notes: Morning produce market until about 2pm, then resets as an aperitivo square by early evening. The brooding statue in the center is Giordano Bruno, burned here for heresy in 1600.

days.cluster_tagline: The imperial heart - Colosseum, Forum, Palatine in one continuous archaeological park
days.neighborhood_description: The southeastern crook of central Rome where the ancient city was governed and entertained. The Colosseum, Forum, and Palatine share a single ticket and a single archaeological zone covering nearly a square kilometer.

morning.notes: Book the timed-entry ticket online days in advance - walk-ups routinely face 2-hour queues. The combined Colosseum/Forum/Palatine ticket is valid for 24 hours so you don't have to do everything today, but most people do. The arena floor access is a separate ticket and worth it if you can get one.

afternoon.notes: Enter from Via dei Fori Imperiali, not the Colosseum side - the elevation walking down through the arches is the cinematic approach. Bring water; there's almost no shade and the marble paths reflect heat.

evening.activity_title: Piazza del Campidoglio and Capitoline views
evening.notes: Michelangelo's piazza on the Capitoline Hill - climb the cordonata ramp rather than the steep stairs to its right. Walk around the back of the Palazzo Senatorio for one of the best free views of the Forum, especially at golden hour.

if_time_permits.notes: The world's oldest public museum (founded 1471). The original bronze Marcus Aurelius equestrian statue lives here - the one in the piazza outside is a replica. Good rainy-day swap for the Forum if weather collapses.

days.cluster_tagline: The world's smallest country and the largest church in Christendom
days.neighborhood_description: An independent state inside Rome, reached by walking west across the Tiber. The Vatican Museums, Sistine Chapel, and St. Peter's together form one of the most visited religious and cultural sites on earth.

morning.notes: Book online at the official museum site weeks ahead - walk-up lines routinely exceed 3 hours in season. The standard route funnels everyone toward the Sistine Chapel at the end; once inside the chapel you can exit directly to St. Peter's if you go right.

afternoon.notes: Entry is free but the security line in St. Peter's Square can be long. Dress code is enforced - shoulders and knees covered, no exceptions. The dome climb is a separate ticket worth buying at the entrance.

evening.activity_title: Castel Sant'Angelo and the bridge at sunset
evening.notes: Hadrian's mausoleum turned papal fortress turned museum. Even if you skip the interior, the Ponte Sant'Angelo with its ten Bernini-designed angels lining the bridge is one of the best sunset walks in Rome, with the dome of St. Peter's at your back and the river bending below.

if_time_permits.notes: The Mussolini-era boulevard cut through the medieval Borgo district to give a monumental approach to St. Peter's. Architecturally controversial but the dome reveal walking west is genuinely cinematic.

days.cluster_tagline: Cobblestone streets, medieval mosaics, and Rome's most beloved neighborhood
days.neighborhood_description: Across the Tiber south of the Vatican, Trastevere is Rome's most atmospheric neighborhood - narrow medieval lanes, ivy-covered facades, and a working-class history that still flickers through the now-gentrified bars and trattorias.

morning.notes: One of Rome's oldest churches with a continuous tradition of public worship since the 3rd century. The 12th-century apse mosaic depicting Mary enthroned beside Christ is the centerpiece - visit when sunlight is angling in from the east for the gold to come alive.

afternoon.notes: A Renaissance villa with frescoes by Raphael - far less crowded than the Vatican but with comparable artistic firepower. The Loggia of Cupid and Psyche and the Triumph of Galatea are the highlights. Closed Sundays except the second Sunday of the month.

evening.notes: Not one of the seven hills, but the best panoramic view of Rome - the entire historic skyline from the Vittoriano to St. Peter's spreads east. A cannon is fired at noon every day from below the terrace, a tradition since 1847 to synchronize the city's church bells.

if_time_permits.notes: Stefano Maderno's eerily lifelike sculpture of the martyred saint lies beneath the altar - modeled, according to legend, on the position her incorrupt body was found in when the tomb was opened in 1599. The Cavallini fresco in the nuns' choir requires a small fee and limited morning hours.

days.cluster_tagline: Rome's grand park, a world-class gallery, and the elegant streets that descend toward the Tiber
days.neighborhood_description: The northeast quadrant of central Rome where the city's grandest park meets the elegant grid of the Tridente - three streets fanning south from Piazza del Popolo that have been Rome's most fashionable address since the 17th century.

morning.notes: Mandatory advance booking with timed entry - the museum caps each two-hour slot at a few hundred people, which is why it stays one of Rome's most pleasant gallery experiences. Bernini's Apollo and Daphne and David are here, along with Caravaggios and the Canova Pauline Bonaparte. The two-hour limit is strictly enforced.

afternoon.activity_title: Pincio Terrace and Villa Borghese gardens
afternoon.notes: The Pincio terrace overlooks Piazza del Popolo with a long-distance view across the rooftops to St. Peter's dome. The Villa Borghese gardens spread north and east - bike rentals near the entrance if you want to cover ground.

evening.activity_title: Spanish Steps and Piazza di Spagna
evening.notes: Descend from the Pincio to the top of the Spanish Steps for the view down toward Piazza di Spagna. Sitting on the steps has been banned and is enforced with a small fine - the locals walk down them and use the piazza below. Keats died in the small pink house at the bottom right; it's now a museum.

if_time_permits.notes: The northern entrance to historic Rome - a giant oval piazza with an Egyptian obelisk at its center and twin baroque churches on its south side. The Santa Maria del Popolo basilica off the northeast corner contains two of Caravaggio's most powerful paintings (the Conversion of Saul and Crucifixion of Peter) hung in their original chapel.
